name: Organophosphate / Nerve Agent Exposure (Atropine + 2-PAM)
one_liner: Cholinergic toxidrome: decon before the doors, atropine until the chest is dry, 2-PAM early and slow — and protect your staff.
indications: Cholinergic toxidrome after pesticide or nerve-agent exposure: DUMBELS (defecation, urination, miosis, bronchorrhea/bronchospasm/bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation)
The killer B's drive treatment urgency: Bronchorrhea, Bronchospasm, Bradycardia
Miosis + secretions + respiratory distress after any chemical incident: treat as nerve agent/OP until proven otherwise
contraindications: None to atropine in a true cholinergic crisis — under-dosing is the error that kills
Pralidoxime: give WITH atropine, never alone; slow the infusion for hypertension/laryngospasm

time_target.label: Decontamination before the patient crosses the doors; atropine within minutes for the killer B's; 2-PAM early, before enzyme aging locks in.

team_calls: Hazmat / decon team — set up outside
Public health + hospital incident command; security to enforce the decon route
Pharmacy — atropine supplies exhaust fast; pool the hospital's stock early
Multiple patients -> declare MCI (see the MCI module)
equipment: Staff PPE before contact: double nitrile gloves, fluid-resistant gown, eye protection (respiratory protection per hazmat guidance)
Decon station OUTSIDE: clothing removal (removes ~80-90% of contamination), copious water +/- soap, runoff containment, bags for belongings
Atropine — in quantity; pralidoxime; midazolam; DuoDote autoinjectors if stocked
Suction (secretions are copious), oxygen, BVM, ventilator

steps.detail: Nobody touches the patient without PPE. Strip all clothing and decon with copious water OUTSIDE before entry — contaminated patients create contaminated staff (Tokyo sarin: ~10% of hospital staff developed symptoms). Bag belongings; contain runoff.

steps.action: Resus room: airway, oxygen, access, monitor
steps.detail: High-flow O2, aggressive suction of secretions, large-bore IV, monitor. Bag-valve ventilation as needed — hypoxia from bronchorrhea/bronchospasm is the immediate threat.

steps.action: Atropine NOW — titrate to the chest, not the pupils
steps.detail: Adult 2 mg IV (3-5 mg if severe); pediatric 0.05 mg/kg. DOUBLE the dose every 5 minutes until secretions dry and air entry is adequate. Endpoints are a dry chest and oxygenation — never pupil size or heart rate. Cumulative needs can reach tens of milligrams; start an infusion (10-20% of the loading dose per hour).

Adult 1-2 g in 100 mL NS IV over 15-30 min (WHO: 30 mg/kg), then 8 mg/kg/h infusion. Pediatric 25-50 mg/kg (max 2 g) then 10-20 mg/kg/h. Reactivates acetylcholinesterase before aging; rapid push causes hypertension and laryngospasm. Give even if presentation is delayed — aging windows vary by agent.

steps.action: Seizures: benzodiazepines, IM if no line
steps.detail: Midazolam 10 mg IM (peds 0.2 mg/kg, max 10 mg) or diazepam IV. Low threshold in severe nerve-agent toxicity — nonconvulsive status is common.
Succinylcholine is metabolized by the same plasma cholinesterase the poison inhibits: paralysis lasts hours. Use rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg.
DuoDote (atropine 2.1 mg + pralidoxime 600 mg) IM lateral thigh, through clothing if needed: mild 1, moderate 2, severe 3 in rapid succession.
Secretions, air entry, oxygenation, fasciculations, mental status every 5 minutes; re-dose atropine on schedule until dry.

steps.action: Zoom out: this is rarely one patient
steps.detail: Chemical event -> notify poison control, public health, hazmat; lock the ambulance bay to force the decon route; declare MCI and open the MCI workflow if more are coming.

meds.name: Atropine
meds.role: Antidote — muscarinic antagonist; dries secretions, breaks bronchospasm/bradycardia

adult.text: 2 mg IV (3-5 mg if severe); DOUBLE every 5 min until secretions dry + air entry adequate; then infusion at 10-20% of loading dose/hr

peds.text: 0.05 mg/kg IV (severe; 0.02 mg/kg mild), double every 5 min to the same endpoints

cautions: Endpoint = dry secretions and adequate air entry. Pupils and heart rate are NOT endpoints.
Expect to use far more than cardiac-arrest doses — pool pharmacy stock early.

meds.name: Pralidoxime (2-PAM)
meds.role: Antidote — reactivates acetylcholinesterase (give before agent-specific aging completes)

adult.text: 1-2 g in 100 mL NS IV over 15-30 min (WHO: 30 mg/kg bolus), then 8 mg/kg/h infusion

peds.text: 25-50 mg/kg IV over 30 min (max 2 g), then 10-20 mg/kg/h infusion

cautions: Rapid infusion -> hypertension, laryngospasm, rigidity — slow the rate
Delayed presentation is not a reason to withhold; aging varies by agent

meds.name: Midazolam
meds.role: Seizure control / prophylaxis in severe toxicity
meds.route: IM (works without a line) or IV

adult.text: 10 mg IM (or 5 mg IV, repeat as needed)

notes: Nonconvulsive status is common in severe nerve-agent toxicity — treat the twitching patient

meds.name: Rocuronium
meds.role: RSI paralytic of choice (succinylcholine is contraindicated in practice)
cautions: Succinylcholine is hydrolyzed by plasma cholinesterase — the enzyme the poison inhibits; its block lasts hours. Use rocuronium.

meds.name: DuoDote autoinjector
meds.role: Field/stopgap antidote when IV therapy lags
meds.route: IM, lateral thigh (through clothing if necessary)

adult.text: Atropine 2.1 mg + pralidoxime 600 mg per injector: mild 1, moderate 2, severe 3 in rapid succession

notes: Military kits add a CANA (diazepam 10 mg) autoinjector with the third DuoDote

pitfalls: Staff contamination — treating before decon turns responders into patients (documented in the Tokyo sarin attack).
Timid atropine dosing — milligram-level caution while the patient drowns in secretions; double every 5 minutes to effect.
Using pupils or heart rate as the atropine endpoint — the chest is the endpoint.
Pushing 2-PAM fast, or giving it without atropine.
Succinylcholine for RSI — hours of paralysis.
Forgetting seizure treatment/prophylaxis in the severe case.
Treating it as a single-patient event — no notification, no MCI declaration, no pharmacy heads-up on atropine stock.
success_criteria: No secondary staff exposures; decon done before entry
Secretions dried, air entry adequate, oxygenation restored (atropinization achieved)
Poison control/public health notified; MCI declared if multiple patients
aftercare: Atropine infusion with slow wean; observe for re-cholinergic rebound
Watch for intermediate syndrome (proximal weakness, respiratory failure) at 24-96 h — extended observation/admission
Carbamate nuance: same acute treatment; discuss 2-PAM duration with toxicology
Staff exposure surveillance; restock antidotes; after-action report

references.label: Eddleston 2008
references.cite: Eddleston M, Buckley NA, Eyer P, Dawson AH. Management of acute organophosphorus pesticide poisoning. Lancet. 2008;371(9612):597-607.

references.label: ATSDR MMG — Nerve Agents
references.cite: ATSDR Medical Management Guidelines for Nerve Agents: Tabun (GA), Sarin (GB), Soman (GD), and VX.

references.label: CHEMM
references.cite: Chemical Hazards Emergency Medical Management (CHEMM), US HHS — nerve agent antidote and DuoDote dosing guidance. chemm.hhs.gov

references.label: Okumura 1998 — Tokyo sarin
references.cite: Okumura T, et al. The Tokyo subway sarin attack: disaster management. Acad Emerg Med. 1998;5(6):613-628. (Secondary staff exposure lessons.)

references.label: Tintinalli 9e
references.cite: Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine, 9th ed. Pesticides / Chemical Disasters.

review.author: Claude (Fable 5), HALO T2 — pending physician review

drill.stem: Explosion at an agricultural chemical warehouse. EMS arrives with a seizing patient soaked in liquid — pinpoint pupils, drooling, diffuse wheeze, HR 48. Two more ambulances are behind them, and staff in street scrubs are reaching for the gurney. (Synthetic scenario.)

decision_points.prompt: Your very first order — before anyone touches the patient?
decision_points.ideal: Stop: staff into PPE; strip and decon the patient with copious water OUTSIDE before entry; bag belongings; contain runoff.

decision_points.prompt: First antidote: drug, starting dose, and titration endpoint?
decision_points.ideal: Atropine 2-5 mg IV (peds 0.05 mg/kg), doubling every 5 minutes until secretions dry and air entry is adequate — the chest is the endpoint, never the pupils.

decision_points.prompt: Second antidote: drug and how it must be given?
decision_points.ideal: Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1-2 g IV over 15-30 minutes (peds 25-50 mg/kg, max 2 g), always with atropine, then an infusion — slow, because rapid push causes hypertension and laryngospasm.

decision_points.prompt: The patient is still seizing and has no IV yet. Drug and route?
decision_points.ideal: Midazolam 10 mg IM (peds 0.2 mg/kg IM, max 10 mg).

decision_points.prompt: You decide to intubate. Which paralytic, and why that one?
decision_points.ideal: Rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg. Succinylcholine is broken down by plasma cholinesterase — the enzyme the poison has inhibited — so its paralysis lasts hours.

decision_points.prompt: You have DuoDote autoinjectors. For this severe patient: how many, and where?
decision_points.ideal: Three, IM into the lateral thigh in rapid succession (through clothing if necessary).

decision_points.prompt: Two more ambulances are inbound. Beyond this patient, what do you set in motion?
decision_points.ideal: Declare an MCI, notify poison control / public health / hazmat, lock the bay to force the decon route, and have pharmacy pool atropine stock.
